coredata.dc:title: Azo dye-based on-site assay paper kit for rapid and selective naked-eye and smartphone-assisted detection of deferoxamine drug

coredata.dc:description: Ferric ion {Fe(III)} is an integral part of many structural and functional components in the body and plays critical roles in physiological and pathological processes. High Fe(III) concentrations damage many systems and organs in the body. The most common method used to prevent excessive accumulation of Fe(III) ions is chelation therapy and the most commonly used drug is deferoxamine (DFO). In this study, an azo dye-based test-paper probe was developed for the colorimetric determination of DFO. The bi-dental azo-dye, named DAZS (diazosalicylaldehyde), was synthesized with a 58 % yield as due to the coupling reaction of 4,4′-diaminobibenzyl and salicylaldehyde. The surface of cellulose-based chromatographic papers immersed in DAZS solution was coated with DAZS and peraped DAZS-coated paper (DAZS-paper) was used as a sensor kit. The structure of DAZS, DAZS-paper, and its interaction mechanism with Fe(III) and DFO were elucidated by FTIR, 1H NMR, 13C NMR, STEM, EDX, and AFM techniques. One arm of DAZS, a symmetrical molecule, interacts with the cellulose-based chromatographic paper, while the other arm remains exposed. This exposed arm interacts with the Fe(III), interrupting the intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) mechanism present in the DAZS molecule, and the color of the DAZS-paper becomes lighter. This interaction, which forms the Fe(III) sensing strategy, is disrupted in the presence of DFO. As a result of its strong chelating feature, DFO forms a chelate complex with Fe(III), enabling the separation of Fe(III) from DAZS. With the separation of the Fe(III), the ICT mechanism is activated again in the free part of DAZS, providing the coloration of DAZS-paper. Although the observed coloration is directly proportional to the concentration of DFO, color analysis was performed with a smartphone via the "colorimeter" application. By analyzing the test paper and solution medium with a smartphone, the detection limits (LOD) of DFO were found to be 95.0 nmol L−1 and 82.0 nmol L−1, respectively. In the selectivity study conducted with various Fe(III) chelating agents, DFO was determined with high selectivity. It was found that common metal cations and anions did not significantly affect the developed probe. The developed method, which was verified by a chromatographic reference method, can also be easily applied to the commercial drug Desferal and fetal bovine serum containing DFO.

1H NMR spectra of chromogenic probe DAZS, DAZS-Fe(III), and DAZS-Fe(III) + DFO in DMSO‑d 6; a) free DAZS, b) DAZS + 1.0 eq-Fe(III), c) DAZS + 2.0 eq-Fe(III), d) DAZS + 2.0 eq-Fe(III) + 1.0 eq-DFO, and e) DAZS + 2.0 eq-Fe(III) + 2.0 eq-DFO.

a) Smartphone color intensity spectra of DAZS-paper sensor containing Fe(III) with various concentrations of DFO (0.0–40.0 × 10−6 mol L−1 at final conc.). b) Calibration plot of DAZS-paper-Fe(III) in the presence of DFO (5.0–40.0 × 10−6 mol L−1 at final conc.). head.ce:title: Azo dye-based on-site assay paper kit for rapid and selective naked-eye and smartphone-assisted detection of deferoxamine drug

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ferric ion {Fe(III)} is an integral part of many structural and functional components in the body and plays critical roles in physiological and pathological processes. High Fe(III) concentrations damage many systems and organs in the body. The most common method used to prevent excessive accumulation of Fe(III) ions is chelation therapy and the most commonly used drug is deferoxamine (DFO). In this study, an azo dye-based test-paper probe was developed for the colorimetric determination of DFO. The bi-dental azo-dye, named DAZS (diazosalicylaldehyde), was synthesized with a 58 % yield as due to the coupling reaction of 4,4′-diaminobibenzyl and salicylaldehyde. The surface of cellulose-based chromatographic papers immersed in DAZS solution was coated with DAZS and peraped DAZS-coated paper (DAZS-paper) was used as a sensor kit. The structure of DAZS, DAZS-paper, and its interaction mechanism with Fe(III) and DFO were elucidated by FTIR, 1H NMR, 13C NMR, STEM, EDX, and AFM techniques. One arm of DAZS, a symmetrical molecule, interacts with the cellulose-based chromatographic paper, while the other arm remains exposed. This exposed arm interacts with the Fe(III), interrupting the intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) mechanism present in the DAZS molecule, and the color of the DAZS-paper becomes lighter. This interaction, which forms the Fe(III) sensing strategy, is disrupted in the presence of DFO. As a result of its strong chelating feature, DFO forms a chelate complex with Fe(III), enabling the separation of Fe(III) from DAZS. With the separation of the Fe(III), the ICT mechanism is activated again in the free part of DAZS, providing the coloration of DAZS-paper. Although the observed coloration is directly proportional to the concentration of DFO, color analysis was performed with a smartphone via the "colorimeter" application. By analyzing the test paper and solution medium with a smartphone, the detection limits (LOD) of DFO were found to be 95.0 nmol L−1 and 82.0 nmol L−1, respectively. In the selectivity study conducted with various Fe(III) chelating agents, DFO was determined with high selectivity. It was found that common metal cations and anions did not significantly affect the developed probe. The developed method, which was verified by a chromatographic reference method, can also be easily applied to the commercial drug Desferal and fetal bovine serum containing DFO.

DAZS-paper, a double-armed chromogenic sensor based on azo dye-loaded on cellulosic paper, interacts with Fe(III) via –OH and –CHO groups and its characteristic orange color changes to light yellow. When DAZS-paper interacted with Fe(III) is immersed in DFO solution, DFO chelate Fe(III) and releases –OH and –CHO groups of DAZS and causes DAZS-paper to become orange color again. This cycle can be effectively repeated 4-times with the same DAZS-paper.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal ions are a fundamental part of many structural and functional components of the body, and these ions play important roles in physiological and pathological processes [1]. Fe(III) ion, which is considered the most important representative among these ions, is essential for the proper functioning of critical biological processes such as hemoglobin production, DNA synthesis, energy metabolism, cell growth and differentiation, electron transfer and oxygen transport [2]. If Fe(III) accumulates excessively in the body, it can accumulate in vital organs such as the liver, heart, pancreas and endocrine glands, damaging the DNA structure and cell membrane [3,4]. This can progress to cell death. If left untreated, excessive iron accumulation can lead to serious diseases such as liver cirrhosis, cancer, arthritis, heart failure, retinal degeneration, diabetes, and neurological diseases (such as Alzheimer's, Parkinson's and Huntington's diseases) [5,6]. Excessive Fe(II) accumulation in the body, in addition to Fe(III), causes Fenton reactions to occur, and the Fenton reaction causes the production of hydroxyl radicals and Fe(III) ions [7].

Chelation therapy is used to prevent or treat damage caused by excessive Fe(III) ion acumulation in the living organism [8]. The basis of the treatment is the use of ligands that will selectively chelate the Fe(III). Chelating agents such as DFO, deferiprone, and deferasirox are used clinically in chelation therapy due to their high affinity for Fe(III) [9,10]. The first and most used chelator is DFO. DFO or desferrioxamine is a trihydroxamic acid siderophore discovered in 1950 as a metabolite of Streptomyces pilosus, a soil bacterium [11,12].

A siderophore (siderophore means iron sign in Greek) is an Fe(III) chelator produced by microorganisms to scavenge the metal ion from inorganic and biological sources and deliver it to the mole where it is recognized and transported across cell membranes [13,14]. More than 500 different siderophores were reported, of which 270 were well characterized, while the rest remain uncharacterized and their functions are yet to be determined. Depending on the moiety structure, siderophores can be classified as hydroxamate, carboxylate, catecholate, and phenolate [15]. DFO belongs to the hydroxamate class as it presents a linear acyclic structure with trihydrosamic functional groups [16].

There is no mechanism in the human body to eliminate excess iron, and the metal accumulates in organs, especially the heart, and liver with truly devastating toxic effects [17,18]. Since the introduction of chelation therapy, the quality of life of patients experiencing Fe(III) accumulation has improved [19]. The extremely high affinity of DFO for the Fe(III) comes from the fact that its linear structure contains a multidentate hydroxamic functional group that surrounds the ferric ion to form a neutral and very stable octahedral complex [20]. Although no explanation was made about the major side effects of DFO in the years when it was first used, serious toxic effects of the drug have been revealed with increasing research over the years [21]. Side effects such as auditory and neurotoxic abnormalities may occur with long-term or high-dose use of DFO and in patients with low iron accumulation [22,23]. For all these reasons, determining DFO in living organisms becomes extremely important.

Azo group dyes (monoazo, diazo, triazo, and polyazo) are considered to be the largest groups of chemical dyes currently in existence (about 26,000 dyes). Azo colorants are the most important textile colorants classified as azo dyes (soluble) and azo pigments (insoluble) according to their solubility [24,25]. They are synthesized from arylamines by diazotation and coupling and characterized by the azo group (R1–NN–R2) [26,27]. Azo compounds are widely used in numerous applications, including pH sensors, the detection of ions and molecules, due to their easy availability, ease of synthesis, wide color gamut, and the sensitivity to environmental change [28–31]. The UV–Vis absorption maxima of azo dyes are sensitive to changes in the environment, and therefore these dyes can also be used as a probe to study molecular events such as solute-solvent interaction, host-guest interaction, and molecular recognition [32–34].

Here, the DAZS-paper kit was developed for a practical application of the colorimetric DFO determination. DAZS was synthesized as a double-arm sensor using a coupling reaction. By immersing cellulosic chromatographic papers into DAZS solution, DAZS was ensured to remain immobile on the paper surface and DAZS-paper kits were obtained. When orange-colored DAZS-paper is immersed in Fe(III) solution, the free region of DAZS and Fe(III) form a complex and the color of DAZS is decreased. When this paper {DAZS-paper-Fe(III)} is immersed in DFO solution, DFO forms a selective chelate complex with Fe(III) and removes the ion from the paper surface. In this way, the color of DAZS-paper turns back to orange color. The interactions of DAZS with Fe(III) and DFO were confirmed by FTIR and 1H NMR titration, and a selectivity study was carried out with different chelators. The effects of different metal cations and anions on the proposed method were examined and DFO was recovered by additive fetal bovine serum. Finally, the commercial Desferal drug and the active ingredient DFO were determined by reference LC-MS/MS method and the developed method, and the method was statistically compared.

The paper-sensor based method developed for DFO has advantages over the reported methods. (i) DAZS-paper is easy to prepare and practical to apply. (ii) Smartphone-assisted determination can be performed without the need for any expensive instruments. (iii) DAZS-paper can be reused up to 4-repetitions. (iv) DAZS-paper is selective only for DFO among different chelators. (v) DAZS-paper can be easily used in biological fluid samples.

Deferoxamine mesylate (DFO), salicylaldehyde, ammonium acetate (NH4CH3COO), 2-amino-2-(hydroxymethyl)-1,3-propanediol (Tris), 8-hydroxyquinoline (8HQ), 5-sulfosalicylic acid dihydrate (SSA), citric acid (anhydrous, CA), and hydrochloric acid (HCl) were purchased from Sigma (St. Louis, Missouri, USA). 1,10–phenanthroline (o-phen, PT), and ethanol (EtOH) were obtained from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, Missouri, USA). 4,4′-diaminonibenzyl, 2,2′-bipyridyl (BP), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), chloride salts of metal cations (FeCl3·6H2O, MnCl2, CaCl2, FeCl2, ZnCl2, CoCl2, MgCl2, CuCl2, CrCl3, AsCl3), and sodium salts of anions (NaCl, Na2CO3, NaNO3, NaNO2) were obtained from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Chromatography paper (0.34 mm Whatman medium thickness 3 MM Chr paper) were purchased from Whatman (Kent, United Kingdom). Desferal (deferoxamine mesylate, 5.0 × 10−1 g vial) drug purchased from Novartis (Basel, Switzerland). All chemicals used were of analytical reagent grade.

Tris–HCl buffer (at pH 4.0–9.0) solutions were prepared from 1.0 × 10−3 mol L−1 Tris (2-amino-2-(hydroxymethyl)-1,3-propanediol) and 1.0 × 10−3 mol L−1 HCl. All aqueous solutions used in the experiments were prepared with ultra–pure water (resistivity 18.2 MΩ cm, Millipore).

UV–Vis spectra were collected in matched Suprasil quartz cuvettes (Hellma Analytics, USA) on a UV–1800 UV–Vis spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan) monitored at 300–800 nm. 1H NMR (300 MHz) and 13C NMR (75 MHz) spectra were recorded on a Unity spectrometer (Varian, USA) in DMSO‑d 6 using TMS as an internal standard. Scanning–transmission electron microscopy (STEM) and local energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) measurements of DAZS-papers were performed using a Quanta 450 FEG scanning electron microscope (FEI, USA). Attenuated total reflectance–Fourier transform infrared (ATR–FTIR) spectra were recorded by a Cary 630 FTIR spectrometer (Agilent, USA) in the 4000–400 cm−1 range. All pH values were measured using a Seven Compact S220 pH meter (Mettler Toledo, Switzerland). WiseStir MSH–20D heater with magnetic stirring (Wisd, Germany) was used for the synthesis of DAZS. The developed method was validated against a literature LC–MS/MS method utilizing a LC–20A liquid chromatograph (Shimadzu, Japan) coupled with a LCMS–8040 mass spectrometer (Shi-madzu, Japan). LC was equipped with an Ultra-AQ column (Restek, USA – 100 × 2.1 mm, 3 μm, C18). Monitoring of color changes observed on the paper surface and in the solution were performed with the Galaxy A51 smartphone (Samsung, South Korea) and the Colorimeter application (application name: Colorimeter; for an Android Smartphone; version: 2.25.16; Lab Tools, Brazil) was used for color analysis.

As seen in Scheme 1 , the diazosalicylaldehyde {DAZS, 5,5’-((ethane-1,2-diylbis(4,1-phenylene))bis(diazene-2,1-diyl))bis(2-hydroxybenzaldehyde)} were prepared according to the modified literature procedure [35] from 4,4′-diaminobibenzyl (0.30 g, 1.40 mmol), NaNO2 (1.00 g, 14.50 mmol) in ultrapure water, and HCl (37 %, 500.0 μL). This mixture was stirred for 1 h in an ice bath. Then, a solution of NaOH (20 %, 3.00 g) and salicylaldehyde (0.35 g, 2.80 mmol) in H2O/MeOH (15.0 mL/10.0 mL) was slowly added to the above solution, which was stirred for 4 h at 0 °C. The precipitate was collected, which was recrystallized in EtOH to afford the desired product as the orange powder (C28H22N4O4, Yield: 58 %, M.p.: 178 °C).

DAZS-papers were prepared using the impregnation technique. White colored chromatography papers cut in appropriate sizes (1.0 cm × 1.0 cm) were left in the DAZS solution (1.0 × 10−2 mol L−1) in MeOH. Chromatography papers were left for the surface to fully interact with the azo dye for a duration of 30 min. At the end of the period, these papers removed from the DAZS solution and kept in a vacuum oven for 5 min to remove the solvent. The orange-colored papers obtained at the end of the period were dipped into the Fe(III) solution (10.0 × 10−4 mol L−1 at final conc.) prepared in ultrapure water. The color of DAZS-papers left in the Fe(III) solution for 10 min turned pale orange/yellow after that time. DAZS-paper-Fe(III) were made ready for DFO determination.

DAZS-papers activated with Fe(III) (10.0 × 10−4 mol L−1 at final conc.) were dipped in DFO solutions prepared at different concentrations (0.0–40.0 × 10−6 mol L−1 at final conc.). DAZS-paper-Fe(III) that were kept in DFO solution for 10 min were taken from DFO solutions and dried in a vacuum oven. In the specially prepared setup, the color of each paper was measured with a smartphone application (Colorimeter application for an Android Smartphone) using a Samsung smartphone (Galaxy A51).

DFO determination was also carried out in solution medium to confirm the results obtained with DAZS-papers. A solution of DAZS (100.0 μL, 1.0 × 10−5 mol L−1), Fe(III) (100.0 μL, 5.0 × 10−5 mol L−1) and Tris-HCl buffer solution (100.0 μL, 1.0 × 10−3 mol L−1, at pH 7.0) were added to a series of vials. Increasing amounts of DFO solution (0.0–50.0 × 10−6 mol L−1 at final conc.) were then added to these solutions. The final volumes of the solutions were made up to 2.0 mL with a MeOH–H2O (9:1, v/v) solvent mixture. The solutions were kept at room temperature for 1 min and the color change in each solution was measured using the smartphone application (Colorimeter application for an Android Smartphone) using a Samsung smartphone (Galaxy A51). In addition, the UV–vis spectrum of each solution was taken.

Firstly, DAZS-papers interacted with Fe(III) (10.0 × 10−4 mol L−1 at final conc.) were dipped in different Fe(III)-chelating agent solutions (40.0 × 10−6 mol L−1 at final conc.; i.e. DFO, 8-HQ, BP, SSA, CA, and PT). The papers were kept in chelating solutions for 10 min and dried in a vacuum oven. Color changes in all papers were measured in the smartphone application (Colorimeter application for an Android Smartphone) using a Samsung smartphone (Galaxy A51).

Possible interference effects of DAZS-papers on interactions Fe(III) (10.0 × 10−4 mol L−1 at final conc.) and DAZS-paper-Fe(III) on interactions DFO were investigated. Accordingly, DAZS-papers and DAZS-paper-Fe(III) were dipped into 50-fold prepared solutions of metal cations (15.0 × 10−4 mol L−1 at final conc.; Na+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Cr3+, As3+, Ca2+, Fe2+, and Cu2+) and anions (15.0 × 10−4 mol L−1 at final conc.; Cl−, NO2 −, NO3 −, and CO3 2−), respectively. All papers were dried in a vacuum oven and DAZS-papers were immersed in Fe(III) solution (10.0 × 10−4 mol L−1 at final conc.) and DAZS-paper-Fe(III) in DFO solution (30.0 × 10−6 mol L−1 at final conc.) left for 10 min. At the end of the period, all the papers were dried in the vacuum oven again and the color changes on the papers were examined with the smartphone application (Colorimeter application for an Android Smartphone) using a Samsung smartphone (Galaxy A51).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for color analysis using smartphone
ce:section.ce:para: For all color analyses to be performed on the paper surface, a white light cabin, a smartphone (Samsung Galaxy A51), and a color analysis application (Colorimeter application for an Android Smartphone) were used. Chromatography papers ready for measurement were placed in a white light cabin, ensuring that the same light environment was used each time. The smartphone was placed 10 cm away from the papers and measurements were made. The data obtained was monitored with the Colorimeter application.

1H NMR titrations were performed on a 300 MHz Unity spectrometer at 298 K. The solution of the DAZS (1.0 × 10−2 mol L−1 in DMSO‑d 6) was titrated by adding known quantities of concentrated solution of Fe(III) (1.0 and 2.0 eq.) and DFO (1.0 and 2.0 eq.). The change of chemical shift of the DAZS were monitored. All titrations were repeated at least twice to get consistent values.

To prepare the Desferal stock solution (1.0 × 10−3 mol L−1), 6.5 × 10−3 g of Desferal drug (deferoxamine mesylate, 5.0 × 10−1 g vial, Novartis, Switzerland) in powder form was weighed and dissolved in H2O. The DFO determination procedure was the same as that mentioned above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay of standard addition method applied to fetal bovine serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: The standard addition method was used for the determination of DFO in commercial fetal bovine serum (FBS). The determination of DFO in FBS was performed using chromogenic probe-paper and a smartphone application. First, FBS was diluted 10-fold with H2O to avoid affecting the DFO analysis of serum protein. The developed chromogenic paper-based method was applied to dilute FBS samples with DFO added.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of development method via reference chromatographic method
ce:section.ce:para: LC–MS/MS method [36] was used for comparison of the developed chromogenic paper-probe for DFO detection. The experimental details of the LC–MS/MS assay were given in the “Supporting Information”.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation, and characterization of the DAZS-paper
ce:section.ce:para: DAZS was synthesized in a one-step by following the coupling reaction procedure with a 58 % yield. The molecular structure and purity of DAZS were elucidated by spectroscopic studies including IR, 1H NMR, 13C NMR, and EDX analysis, and microscopic studies including STEM analysis.

When the infrared spectrum given in Fig. 1 a is examined; the phenolic –OH stretching (3348 cm−1) peak, the = C–H stretching (3012 cm−1) peak of the aromatic ring, the –C–H stretching (2993 and 2987 cm−1) and bending (1024 cm−1) peaks of the aliphatic chain, –CO (1695 cm−1) stretching peaks of the aldehyde group, the –NN– bending (1147 and 1462 cm−1) peaks of the azo group, and the –C–N = bending (1109 cm−1) peak at which the aromatic rings are attached to the azo group are visible. The peak observed at 2713 cm−1 of the aldehyde group (–CHO) of DAZS is seen to disappear after the interaction with Fe(III) (Fig. 1b). This supports the interaction of the Fe(III) ion with the aldehyde oxygen of DAZS. In addition, the –OH band centered at 3348 cm−1 of DAZS also becomes broader after the interaction with Fe(III). In the FTIR spectrum of DAZS + Fe(III) + DFO given in Fig. 1c, the aldehyde peak of DAZS is observed again. After the complex formed by DFO with the Fe(III) ion, the aldehyde group of DAZS is released again. The obtained data support our detection mechanism, which will be explained in more detail in the section "3.4. Naked-Eye Mechanism for the Detection of DFO".
1H NMR and 13C NMR analysis of DAZS were also performed for structural characterization. 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectra of DAZS are as given in Figures S1 and S2 in the “Supporting Information”.

DAZS-paper was prepared by the procedure given in section 2.6 as summarized in Fig. 2 a. To characterize the prepared DAZS-papers, STEM images of unloaded chromatography paper, DAZS-loaded chromatography paper, DAZS-paper-Fe(III) and DAZS-paper-Fe(III) + DFO were taken, respectively, and 3D modeling of each image was created using ImageJ program. When the STEM image in Fig. 2b is examined in detail, cellulose fibers and the spaces between these fibers are clearly seen on the unloaded chromatography paper. In 3D modeling, these voids (blue-purple colored pits) and cellulose fibers (yellow-orange hills) confirm this structure. In addition, the C and O peaks seen in the EDX spectrum of the unloaded paper support the cellulose structure of the paper (Figure S3a). In the STEM image of DAZS-loaded paper, it is seen that the pits in the unloaded paper are filled. The small number of blue-violet colored pits in the 3D modeling confirms the loading (Fig. 2c). The presence of the N peak in the EDX spectrum alongside the C and O peaks proves the existence of DAZS on the paper surface (Figure S3b). In Fig. 2d, it is seen that in DAZS-paper-Fe(III), the pits are well filled and the fibrous structure is dominant in the paper. The decrease of pits and hills in the 3D modeling of the STEM image and the presence of Fe and Cl {Cl peak is seen because FeCl3 is used as Fe(III) salt} peaks in the EDX spectrum, as well as C, O, and N peaks support this situation (Figure S3c). Finally, when the surface of the paper interacted with DFO, it is seen that the pits are completely filled and there are reticulate structures between some fibers (Fig. 2e). In 3D modeling, it is understood that the pits are almost absent and C, O, N, and Fe peaks are on the paper surface in the EDX spectrum given in Figure S3d. Al peaks are seen in all recorded EDX spectra. This peak is due to the aluminum plate on which the analysis is made. In addition, the strong presence of the O peak in each spectrum can be attributed to the predominance of –OH groups in the cellulose structure of the paper.

The optimal parameters for DAZS-paper pertaining to the number of washes (Fig. S4a and S4b), the number of detections of DFO in the with and without oxygen environment (Fig. S5a and S5b), the medium-pH (Figure S6a), reaction time (Figure S6b), and mixture with water (Fig. S7a and S7b) are as described in “Supporting Information”.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Binding stoichiometry of DAZS with Fe(III) and DFO
ce:section.ce:para: DAZS is a double-armed compound and therefore can theoretically bind two Fe(III) ions. Job's plot method [37] was applied to calculate this binding stoichiometry. A series of solutions in different volume ratios were prepared from DAZS and Fe(III) solutions with a concentration of 30.0 × 10−6 mol L−1 (at final conc.), with a constant total volume. Absorption spectra of each prepared solution were taken. The absorbance values recorded at 475 nm wavelength were plotted against the mole fraction of Fe(III) (Figure S8a). The highest absorbance value obtained in the graph corresponds to the 0.3 mol fraction of the Fe(III). This ratio indicates that the stoichiometric ratio between metal {Fe(III)} and ligand (DAZS) is 2:1. However, DFO added to the medium {DAZS-Fe(III)} can theoretically bind only a Fe(III). In order to calculate the binding stoichiometry of this situation, the absorption spectra of the solutions prepared by mixing DAZS-Fe(III) {30.0 × 10−6 mol L−1 at final conc. DAZS and Fe(III)} and DFO (30.0 × 10−6 mol L−1 at final conc.) in different volumes, this time with the total volume constant, were recorded. Similarly, the recorded absorbance values were plotted against the mole fraction of DFO (Figure S8b). In the graph, the highest absorbance value corresponds to the 0.5 mol fraction of DFO, which reveals the 1:1 bonding stoichiometry between DFO and Fe(III).

After the stoichiometric binding ratios calculation (using Job's plot) performed in the solution medium, the same calculations were performed in the test paper. DAZS-papers prepared with DAZS solutions prepared in methanol of different concentrations were dipped in Fe(III) solutions prepared at different concentrations (the total concentration was kept constant at 30.0 × 10−6 mol L−1). The color changes were observed in the papers and measured with the "colorimetry" app (using a Samsung Galaxy A51 smartphone) and all values were converted to absorbance with the help of Eq-1.
(Eq −1)
A = − log
(
I
I 0
)
In the equation, A, I, and I
0 represent absorbance, intensity of the sample (DAZS-papers + Fe(III)), and intensity of the reference (DAZS-papers), respectively.

These absorbance values were plotted against the mole fraction of Fe(III) (Figure S9a). The lowest absorbance value observed in the graph corresponds to the 0.5 mol fraction of Fe(III). This shows that the stoichiometric ratio between DAZS and Fe(III) is 1:1. Note that while a DAZS molecule can bind two Fe(III) in a solution medium, the DAZS molecule attached to the paper can only bind a Fe(III). This supports our detection principle outlined in Scheme 2 . When DAZS, which is double-armed, interacts with the paper, only one side is exposed and therefore it can bind a ferric ion in its structure. Finally, the DAZS-paper- Fe(III) were dipped in DFO solutions prepared at different concentrations (the total concentration was kept constant at 30.0 × 10−6 mol L−1). The color changes observed in the prepared papers were measured by the "colorimetry" app and all values were converted to absorbance. When the absorbances plotted against the mole fraction of DFO are analyzed, the highest absorbance value is obtained in the 0.5 mol fraction (Figure S9b). This indicates that the stoichiometric ratio between DFO and Fe(III) is 1:1.

The binding mode of the DAZS compound with Fe(III) was investigated using 1H NMR titration studies. The 1H NMR spectra of the DAZS chemosensor, both before and after the addition of Fe(III), as well as in the presence and absence of DFO in DMSO‑d 6, are presented in Fig. 3 . The most notable spectral changes were observed upon the addition of Fe(III) to the free DAZS sensor solution as follows.
(i)
The peak corresponding to the –OH proton at 11.65 ppm disappeared upon the addition of the Fe(III), likely due to the binding of the oxygen atom to the metal ion, which could lead to deprotonation by weakening the O–H bond [38].
(ii)
The proton signal for the –CHO group, which appeared at 10.35 ppm in free DAZS, was found to shift slightly to 10.3 ppm. Upon the addition of 0.5-equivalents of Fe(III), this signal shifted further to 10.2 ppm as the Fe(III) concentration increased to 1.0-equivalent. This indicates that the aldehyde oxygen is involved in the binding of the ferric ion. The increase in the –CHO proton signal upon binding of Fe(III) to the aldehyde oxygen can be attributed to the partial delocalization of the resulting positive charge through a reversible nucleophilic coupling with a water molecule, leading to the formation of an octahedral intermediate. Similar Fe(III)-promoted nucleophilic attack to the CN, and subsequent hydrolysis have been previously reported [39].
(iii)
Upon the addition of 0.5- and 1.0-equivalents of DFO to the system, the lost –OH proton signal and the –CHO proton signal, which had shifted to higher ppm values, were restored. This suggests that the Fe(III) may be dissociated from the DAZS probe, likely due to the formation of a complex between Fe(III) and DFO. This observation supports the proposed mechanism and confirms the formation of the Fe(III)-DFO complex.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Naked-Eye Mechanism for the detection of DFO
ce:section.ce:para: DAZS is a compound that has more than one conjugated group in its structure. In addition, the phenolic –OH group in its structure may lose its proton depending on the pH of the medium or the polarity of the solvent used. In this case, the excess charge accumulating on the oxygen atom is delocalized with the help of the ICT (intramolecular charge transfer) mechanism [40] and coloration is observed. The pH of the medium was examined in the “optimization” section. Here, the absorption spectra of DAZS in the presence of seven-different solvents with different polarities were collected to observe solvatocromism (i.e. the color of the solute being different when a solute dissolves in different solvents). Solvents were divided into three different groups according to their polarity and hydrogen bonding properties. The group of almost non-polar solvent includes toluene, the dipolar-aprotic group includes solvents such as DMSO, ACN, THF, and acetone. The third category solvents includes MeOH and EtOH, and are called polar protic/hydroxylic solvents.

When the spectra and photographs given in Fig. S10 were examined, it was observed that it was light orange in toluene, a non-polar solvent, and this situation continued in THF and acetone, which are dipolar-aprotic solvents. In the presence of DMSO and acetonitrile in the same group, it was determined that DAZS started to color (orange color) and there was a bathachromic shift in the spectra (Figure S10a). The best coloration of DAZS was observed in the presence of polar-protic solvents EtOH and MeOH (Figure S10b). In addition, a new shoulder was observed at 475 nm in the UV–vis spectra taken in the presence of both solvents. The high-energy dense band is attributed to the π→π∗ transition of the less polar benzenoid form, while the low-intensity shoulder band is attributed to the more polar quinonoid form (Fig. S11a and S11b) [41]. Polar protic solvents can stabilize the quinonoid form more than other solvents increasing the absorbance of the shoulder band due to their higher polarity and H-bonding donor abilities. In addition, the –OH and –CHO groups existing in the structure of DAZS in both polar-protic solvents form H-bonding within itself, which strengthens the formation of the quinonoid form.

As seen in Scheme 2, the developed DAZS-paper-based chromogenic probe for the determination of DFO works with the Fe(III) chelating principle. As a result of the interaction of the Fe(III) with phenolic oxygen (–OH) and aldehyde oxygen (–CHO) of DAZS, the ICT mechanism in the DAZS molecule is interrupted. In this way, DAZS papers are lightened (from orange to pale yellow). By adding DFO to the DAZS-paper-Fe(III), DFO forms a chelate complex {ML; M: Fe(III), L: DFO} with Fe(III) with its strong chelating properties [42]. As a result of this complex formation, the DAZS interaction with the Fe(III) is interrupted. Because DAZS-Fe(III) interaction is weaker than DFO-Fe(III) interaction. With the release of phenol and aldehyde oxygen, the ICT mechanism works again in the DAZS molecule and the DAZS-papers are colored (from pale yellow to orange).

To further investigate the relationship between Fe(III) and different chelators, the formation constants of the complexes formed by Fe(III) with different chelators were examined. When complex formation constants (log K) are examined in detail, as shown in Table 1 , DFO makes the most stable complex with Fe(III). DFO is followed by BP, PT, SSA, CA, and 8HQ, respectively. In the experiments, it is known that only DFO can separate the Fe(III) that forms the DAZS-Fe(III) complex with DAZS. The reason for this situation is that the constant of the complex formed between the possible Fe(III) and DAZS is smaller than that of DFO and larger than that of BP. Thus, other chelators added except DFO cannot separate and chelate the Fe(III) from the DAZS-Fe(III) complex and do not respond to the developed method. This makes DAZS an important player in determining whether DFO is selective in the presence of different chelators.

DAZS-papers developed for the visual determination of DFO were dipped into DFO solution prepared at different concentrations (0.0-40.0 × 10−6 mol L−1). The color changes on these DAZS-papers were analyzed using the “Colorimeter” app. When the spectra given in Fig. 4 a are examined, the color intensities decrease with increasing DFO concentrations at 470 nm wavelength. For a more detailed examination, the color intensity values read at 470 nm wavelength were converted to absorbance using the equation (Eq-1). These absorbance values were plotted against DFO concentration as shown in Fig. 4b and a good linear relationship was obtained in the (5.0–40.0) × 10−6 mol L−1 concentration range of DFO.

The linear equation of the curve was;

where A was absorbance (according to Eq-1) and C DFO was the DFO concentration (in μmol L−1) in the final solution.

As a result of the excellent data obtained with DAZS-paper, a similar interaction was also tested in the solution medium. DAZS solution (50.0 × 10−6 mol L−1 at final conc.) prepared in MeOH solution was added to a series of vials. An equal amount of Fe(III) solution (50.0 × 10−6 mol L−1 at final conc.) was added to these solutions and light yellow solutions were obtained. Then, increasing concentrations of DFO solution {(0.0–50.0) × 10−6 mol L−1 at final conc.} were added to these vials and the final volumes of the solutions were made up to 1.0 mL with MeOH:H2O (9:1, v/v). The same coloration that occurred on DAZS-paper surfaces was also observed in the solution medium. Color changes in these solutions were analyzed by the “Colorimeter” app and the spectra shown in Fig. 4c were obtained. The color intensities read at 468 nm wavelength were converted to absorbance with the help of the equation given in Eq-1 and the calibration graph given in Fig. 4d was created. A linear relationship was obtained in the (10.0–50.0) × 10−6 mol L−1 concentration range of DFO.

where A was absorbance (according to Eq-1) and C DFO was the DFO concentration (in μmol L−1) in the final solution. In addition, the UV–vis spectra of these prepared solutions were recorded. When the spectra given in Fig. S12a were examined, smooth increases were obtained with increasing DFO concentrations {(0.0–50.0) × 10−6 mol L−1 at final conc.} at 475 nm wavelength. However, it is seen that the correlation coefficient (r = 0.9982) of the calibration curve created with the results obtained with the UV–vis spectrophotometer (Figure S12b) is very close to the correlation coefficients (r = 0.9956 for DAZS-paper and r = 0.9952 for DAZS solution) obtained with the smartphone application. The obtained data show that the smartphone-assisted DAZS-paper method is as sensitive as the UV–vis method and that the DAZS-paper method can be easily used in the determination of DFO.

The limits of detection for DFO were 95.0 nmol L−1 for DAZS-paper and 82.0 nmol L−1 for DAZS solution medium (LOD = 3σbl/m, σbl denoting the standard deviation of the blank (10 samples) absorbance ratio, and m showing the slope of the calibration line), respectively. Analytical performance parameters with the (percentage) coefficients of variation (C.V.) of the developed method were given in Table S1 (Supporting Information).

A selectivity study was performed with different Fe(III) chelators (i.e. DFO, 8-HQ, BP, SSA, CA, and PT). DAZS-papers interacted with Fe(III) (1.0 × 10−4 mol L−1) and were dipped in solutions of different Fe(III) chelators. DAZS-papers, which were kept for 10 min, were taken from the solutions at the end of the time and dried in a vacuum oven. When the DAZS-papers were examined, the orange color was observed only in the presence of the DFO chelator, while a slight yellowing was observed in the presence of the remaining chelators. When the spectra recorded by the color analysis with the “Colorimeter” app are examined, the intensity observed in the presence of Fe(III) decreases with DFO and approaches a value close to DAZS-paper (Fig. 5 a). The concentration decreases in the presence of remaining chelators, but this reduction is very insufficient compared to DFO (Fig. 5b).

The reversible binding behavior of molecular sensors towards their selective analytes is one of the important parameters for practical application [46]. In this context, the reversible binding of DAZS with Fe(III) is directly proportional to the DFO present in the medium. DAZS has an orange color that absorption peaks at 470 nm. This color is quenched by immersing DAZS-paper in Fe(III) solution, and the absorbance value at 470 nm shows a serious decrease. When this DAZS-paper-Fe(III) is immersed in DFO solution, the formation of DFO and Fe(III) complex causes the release of DAZS and return of the orange color. A rhythmic repetition is observed, which includes lightning the color again as a result of immersing the free DAZS-paper in Fe(III) and returning the color when it enters the DFO solution (Fig. 6 ). All these results indicate that DAZS can be used as a reversible chromogenic sensor for the selective detection of DFO.

Interference experiments were carried out to examine the effect of different metal cations (50-fold; Na+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Cr3+, As3+, Ca2+, Fe2+, and Cu2+) and anions (50-fold; Cl−, NO2 −, NO3 −, and CO3 2−) for the developed method. The effects of different metal cations and anions on the DAZS-paper-Fe(III) and DAZS-paper-Fe(III) + DFO were measured with a smartphone. When the bar graph in Fig. 7 is examined, it is seen that both metal cations and anions do not have a significant effect on both systems within the significance level (α = 0.05). It is possible to say that, in addition to the signal-enhancing effect of metal cations, anions have a signal-decreasing effect due to their possible interaction with the Fe(III). The recovery values obtained in the presence of each interferant are summarized in Table S2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recovery of DFO from fetal bovine serum
ce:section.ce:para: The standard addition method was applied to commercial fetal bovine serum (FBS) samples. FBS samples were diluted 10-fold with ultrapure water to eliminate any influence of proteins present in FBS on the analysis. The developed method was applied to diluted FBS samples by adding different concentrations of DFO (5.0 × 10−6, 10.0 × 10−6, 20.0 × 10−6, 30.0 × 10−6, and 40.0 × 10−6 mol L−1). The absorbance values obtained by the smartphone were compared with the absorbance values obtained by the method applied without FBS samples and the recovery values were calculated. The average recovery percentage of the data in Table S3 was found to be 103.28 ± 1.48.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of DFO in desferal drug
ce:section.ce:para: The developed method was applied to the determination of DFO in Desferal, a chelating drug used in the treatment of excess Fe(III) in the body. When the package insert of Desferal was examined, it was seen that the active ingredient of the drug was DFO and it did not contain any excipients. Therefore, 6.5 × 10−3 g of powdered Desferal was weighed and dissolved in H2O without any pretreatment, and the final volume was completed to 10.0 mL. Five working solutions with a final concentration of 20.0 × 10−6 mol L−1 were prepared from this Desferal stock solution (1.0 × 10−3 mol L−1), and these solutions were analyzed using developed method. The average recovery percentage was found to be 105.34 ± 3.4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of proposed method against chromatographic method
ce:section.ce:para:
The proposed method was applied to both commercial DFO samples and DFO-containing desferal drug samples. First of all, five different DFO solutions with concentration ranges ranging from 5.0 × 10−6 to 40.0 × 10−6 mol L−1 were prepared for the reference chromatographic method [29]. The calibration curve for the reference method was obtained with the prepared solutions. The line equation formed by the peak areas obtained against the DFO concentration is given below.

With the help of the equation created, five-commercial DFO solutions at 15.0 × 10−6 mol L−1 concentration and five desferal drug solutions at 20.0 × 10−6 mol L−1 concentration were prepared. Each solution was applied to both the proposed method and the reference LC-MS/MS method and calculations were made. The average DFO (DFO in both commercial and Desferal drug) values found by the developed and LC-MS/MS methods were statistically compared (n 1 = n 2 = 5) and summarized as shown in Table 2 .

A molecular logic gate is a molecule that takes one or more chemical input signals and produces a single optical output (absorbance or fluorescence intensity) [47]. Molecular logic gates involve chemical processes with molecular recognition input signals and spectroscopy-based output signals [47]. DAZS, a chromogenic sensor, exhibits a “rhythmically” reversible binding behavior upon the addition of Fe(III) and DFO, respectively, and thus the Boolean function can be applied to the working principle of the molecular logic gate sensor. The presence and absence of “Input 1” {Fe(III)} and “Input 2” (DFO) are defined as “1” and “0” respectively. In the presence of these inputs, the formation and disappearance of a strong absorbance at 470 nm is considered as the output of the logic gate. Fig. 8 a shows the circuit of this logic gate. Free DAZS exhibits a strong absorption peak at 470 nm and this output is considered “1”. By adding Fe(III) to the medium, DAZS forms a complex with Fe(III), and its absorbance intensity decreases significantly and this output is accepted as "0". In this case, the system returns to its initial state by adding DFO to the medium; DFO and Fe(III) form a strong complex, releasing DAZS and the absorption peak at 470 nm returns. This output is also considered “1” (Fig. 8b and c). The outputs obtained by different combinations of inputs imitate the IMPLICATION-type logic gate at the molecular level. The Boolean function outputs '1′ in all cases, except {Fe(III)} when a particular input is '1'.

An innovative method was developed for the determination of deferoxamine, a popular chelating agent used for the removal of excess Fe(III) ion, a physiologically important metal cation, both on paper surface and in solution. The design of the sensor material DAZS in the method as a double-armed sensor allowed DAZS to be loaded onto the cellulosic paper surface and to interact with the analyte by its free part. This distinguishes DAZS from single-arm sensors reported in the literature. DAZS-paper, which was prepared by successfully imitating the chelation process that occurs in physiological environment, first interacts with Fe(III) ion and the stability value of this supramolecular interaction is higher than the stability values of various Fe(III)-chelators. Thus, DAZS-paper-Fe(III) interaction is interrupted only in the presence of DFO chelator. In addition, all these interactions can be monitored with smartphone support. DFO determination can be performed with high recovery values in the presence of ions frequently found in living organisms, and in fetal bovine serum samples. It is believed that this simple, selective, and easy-to-implement colorimetric sensor (not requiring highly trained personnel and advanced instrumentation) for precisely measuring the biochemically important analyte DFO will find further use in pharmaceutical and medicinal chemistry laboratories.
